title: 6  The Grand Simulation Challenge
text:
6.6 Bayesian RI-CLPM with Informative Priors
The 9:1 between-to-within ratio is exactly the regime where the ML RI-CLPM falls apart: within-person residual variances are tiny relative to the random-intercept variances, the likelihood is flat near the boundary, and we get Heywood cases or non-convergence on a large fraction of replications. Informative priors anchor the variance components away from zero and shrink the AR/CL coefficients toward sensible values, fixing the empirical underidentification without changing the model.
This is a small Monte Carlo demo (50 replications, 9:1 ratio, RI-CLPM DGP). It is intentionally small because Stan compilation/sampling is expensive. The point is to compare ML vs Bayesian behavior in this hard regime.

library(blavaan)

riclpm_syn_bayes_base <- estimateRICLPM(
  waves = waves, constrain_beta = FALSE, constrain_omega = FALSE,
  constrain_residual_variances = FALSE, constrain_residual_covariances = FALSE
)

add_riclpm_priors <- function(syn) {
  syn <- gsub('(ar_x[0-9]*)\\*',  'prior("normal(0.3,0.15)")*\\1*', syn, perl = TRUE)
  syn <- gsub('(ar_y[0-9]*)\\*',  'prior("normal(0.3,0.15)")*\\1*', syn, perl = TRUE)
  syn <- gsub('(cl_xy[0-9]*)\\*', 'prior("normal(0,0.1)")*\\1*',    syn, perl = TRUE)
  syn <- gsub('(cl_yx[0-9]*)\\*', 'prior("normal(0,0.1)")*\\1*',    syn, perl = TRUE)
  syn <- gsub('(d_var_x[0-9]*)\\*', 'prior("gamma(2,8)")[sd]*\\1*', syn, perl = TRUE)
  syn <- gsub('(d_var_y[0-9]*)\\*', 'prior("gamma(2,8)")[sd]*\\1*', syn, perl = TRUE)
  syn
}

riclpm_syn_bayes <- add_riclpm_priors(riclpm_syn_bayes_base)

set.seed(2026)
n_reps_bayes <- 50
pop_extreme  <- make_riclpm_pop(9)
bayes_file   <- here::here("data", "bayes_summary.rds")

if (!file.exists(bayes_file)) {
  bayes_results <- vector("list", n_reps_bayes)
  for (i in seq_len(n_reps_bayes)) {
    dat <- lavaan::simulateData(pop_extreme, sample.nobs = n_obs)
    ml_warns <- character(0)
    ml_fit <- tryCatch(
      withCallingHandlers(
        lavaan::lavaan(estimateRICLPM(waves = waves), data = dat),
        warning = function(w) { ml_warns <<- c(ml_warns, conditionMessage(w)); invokeRestart("muffleWarning") }
      ),
      error = function(e) NULL
    )
    ml_ok <- !is.null(ml_fit) && lavInspect(ml_fit, "converged")
    ml_heywood <- if (ml_ok) {
      pe <- parameterEstimates(ml_fit)
      any(pe$est[pe$op == "~~" & pe$lhs == pe$rhs] < 0, na.rm = TRUE)
    } else NA

    bayes_error <- NA_character_
    bayes_fit <- tryCatch(
      suppressWarnings(suppressMessages(
        blavaan::bsem(riclpm_syn_bayes, data = dat,
                      n.chains = 2, burnin = 500, sample = 500,
                      bcontrol = list(cores = 2), seed = 1000 + i)
      )),
      error = function(e) { bayes_error <<- conditionMessage(e); NULL }
    )
    bayes_ok <- !is.null(bayes_fit) &&
      isTRUE(tryCatch(blavaan::blavInspect(bayes_fit, "converged"), error = function(e) FALSE))
    bayes_heywood <- if (bayes_ok) {
      pe <- parameterEstimates(bayes_fit)
      any(pe$est[pe$op == "~~" & pe$lhs == pe$rhs] < 0, na.rm = TRUE)
    } else NA

    bayes_results[[i]] <- tibble(
      rep = i, ml_converged = ml_ok, ml_heywood = ml_heywood,
      ml_warnings = length(ml_warns),
      bayes_finished = bayes_ok, bayes_heywood = bayes_heywood,
      bayes_error = bayes_error
    )
  }
  bayes_summary <- bind_rows(bayes_results)
  saveRDS(bayes_summary, bayes_file)
} else {
  bayes_summary <- readRDS(bayes_file)
}

bayes_summary |>
  summarise(
    Replications         = n(),
    `ML converged`       = mean(ml_converged),
    `ML Heywood (of conv.)` = mean(ml_heywood[ml_converged], na.rm = TRUE),
    `ML mean # warnings` = mean(ml_warnings),
    `Bayes finished`     = mean(bayes_finished, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Bayes Heywood (of finished)` = dplyr::if_else(
      sum(bayes_finished, na.rm = TRUE) > 0,
      mean(bayes_heywood[bayes_finished], na.rm = TRUE),
      NA_real_
    )
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Metric", values_to = "Value") |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = Value, decimals = 2) |>
  tab_header(title = "RI-CLPM at 9:1 Between:Within Ratio",
             subtitle = paste0(nrow(bayes_summary), " replications, n = ", n_obs))







RI-CLPM at 9:1 Between:Within Ratio

The ML column shows the underidentification problem in action — most replications either fail to converge or land on a boundary solution with at least one negative variance estimate. The Bayesian column should show every replication completing cleanly with admissible variances. The priors aren’t doing anything exotic: they just rule out the corner of parameter space the ML optimizer keeps wandering into.

title: 7  The ISCAP Panel
text:
7.5 The Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC)
The Analysis of Variance model simply decomposes the total variance for a variable into within and between components. The ICC (\(\rho\)) is the share of total variance attributable to stable between-person differences. We can easily calculate this on the long data using a multilevel model in lme4
\[
    ext{ICC} \;=\; \frac{\sigma^2_{\text{between}}}{\sigma^2_{\text{between}} + \sigma^2_{\text{within}}}
\]

library(lme4)

# Null random-intercept models, one per outcome
m_party <- lmer(party_identification ~ 1 + (1 | identifier), 
REML = FALSE, data = upenn_long)
m_legit <- lmer(political_legitimacy  ~ 1 + (1 | identifier),
REML = FALSE, data = upenn_long)

icc_from_lmer <- function(fit) {
  vc <- as.data.frame(VarCorr(fit))
  between <- vc$vcov[vc$grp == "identifier"]
  within  <- vc$vcov[vc$grp == "Residual"]
  data.frame(
    between_var = between,
    within_var  = within,
    total_var   = between + within,
    ICC         = between / (between + within)
  )
}

icc_tbl <- bind_rows(
  cbind(variable = "Party Identification", icc_from_lmer(m_party)),
  cbind(variable = "Political Legitimacy", icc_from_lmer(m_legit))
)

icc_tbl |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(between_var, within_var, total_var, ICC),
             decimals = 3) |>
  cols_label(
    variable    = "Variable",
    between_var = "Between (σ²_u)",
    within_var  = "Within (σ²_e)",
    total_var   = "Total",
    ICC         = "ICC"
  ) |>
  tab_header(title = "Analysis of Variance Components")

title: 7  The ISCAP Panel
text:
7.6 Validation with the TSO
Another way to estimate these component is with a TSO model.


# Trait factors (between-person)
T_x =~ 1*x1 + 1*x2 + 1*x3 + 1*x4
T_y =~ 1*y1 + 1*y2 + 1*y3 + 1*y4

# Trait variances and covariance
T_x ~~ T_var_x*T_x
T_y ~~ T_var_y*T_y
T_x ~~ T_cov_xy*T_y

# State variances (within-person)
x1 ~~ s_var_x*x1
y1 ~~ s_var_y*y1
x2 ~~ s_var_x*x2
y2 ~~ s_var_y*y2
x3 ~~ s_var_x*x3
y3 ~~ s_var_y*y3
x4 ~~ s_var_x*x4
y4 ~~ s_var_y*y4

# Within-time state covariances
x1 ~~ s_cov_xy*y1
x2 ~~ s_cov_xy*y2
x3 ~~ s_cov_xy*y3
x4 ~~ s_cov_xy*y4


This produces identical ICC estimates.

tso_syntax <- crossLagR::estimateTSO(waves = 4)

tso_data <- upenn |>
  dplyr::select(identifier,
                x1 = party_identification_2012,
                x2 = party_identification_2016,
                x3 = party_identification_2018,
                x4 = party_identification_2020,
                y1 = political_legitimacy_2012,
                y2 = political_legitimacy_2016,
                y3 = political_legitimacy_2018,
                y4 = political_legitimacy_2020) |>
  mutate(across(c(x1, x2, x3, x4), ~ (.x - 1) / 6))

## Use lavaan::sem() rather than lavaan::lavaan(): the bare lavaan() engine
## with meanstructure = TRUE allocates intercept parameters but does NOT free
## the observed-variable intercepts, so variances are computed around 0
## instead of around the variable mean and the trait variance is inflated by
## mean(x)^2. sem() auto-frees them.
tso_fit <- lavaan::sem(tso_syntax, data = tso_data, missing = "ML")

pe <- lavaan::parameterEstimates(tso_fit)
grab <- function(lab) pe$est[pe$label == lab][1]

tibble::tibble(
  Variable    = c("Party Identification", "Political Legitimacy"),
  Trait_var   = c(grab("T_var_x"), grab("T_var_y")),
  State_var   = c(grab("s_var_x"), grab("s_var_y"))
) |>
  mutate(Total_var = Trait_var + State_var,
         ICC       = Trait_var / Total_var) |>

  fmt_number(columns = c(Trait_var, State_var, Total_var, ICC), decimals = 3) |>

title: 7  The ISCAP Panel
text:
7.7 Arellano-Bond Dynamic Panel Model
The Arelleno-Bond estimator uses GMM on the first-differenced data – to remove the unit effects – with lagged variables serving as instruments for the lagged right-hand side differenced variables.
The code below fits two separate models: * Political legitimacy as the outcome and party identification as the predictor * Party identification as the outcome and political legitimacy as the predictor. Both models include a lag of the dependent variable on the right-hand side to capture autoregression. The instrument set includes lags 2 and 3 of the dependent variable, and lag 1 of the predictor variable. This is a strong assumption for party id – typically the Arellano-Bond instrument matrix unfolds such that wave 1 of the predictor is used as an instrument for the change from wave 2 to wave 3, wave 1 and 2 are used to instrument the change from wave 2 to 3, and wave 1, 2, and 3 are used to instrument the change from wave 3 to 4. Party ID is so stable that it is exogenous. The results show a strong autoregressive effect for both variables, but no evidence of cross-lagged effects.


Oneway (individual) effect Two-steps model Difference GMM 

Call:
pgmm(formula = political_legitimacy ~ lag(political_legitimacy, 
    1) + lag(party_identification, 1) | lag(political_legitimacy, 
    2:3) | lag(party_identification, 1), data = ab_data, effect = "individual", 
    model = "twosteps", transformation = "d")

The model is useful, but it does assume something that is problematic in this context: The correlation between the lagged IV and the DV should change from wave to wave; in this case, from election-to-election, with presidents of both political parties. If a Democrat is in office, Republicans should become more critical; if a Republican is in office, Democrats should be more critical.
A similar model, estimated via Maximum Likelihood versus Generalized Method of Moments, is the Bollen and Brand dynamic panel model, which is described in the next section.

title: 7  The ISCAP Panel
section: 7.8 Bollen and Brand Dynamic Panel Model
text:
7.8 Bollen and Brand Dynamic Panel Model
The Bollen and Brand (2010) dynamic panel model captures autoregression, cross-lagged effects, and unobserved heterogeneity (latent fixed effects) within an SEM framework (see also Dishop and DeShon 2018). The diagram below shows the reciprocal cross-lag specification with 4 waves.






Because we freely estimate the AR and CL parameters, there are far more statistically significant coefficients – they just change signs on occasion, which is why they were, on average, non-significant in the Arellano-Bond model. And lagged legitimacy never predicts PID – which makes sense, because PID is stable, but its correlation with legitimacy is not.

# Ensure SEM-wide aliases exist even if chunks are re-ordered.
if (!exists("upenn_sem")) {
  upenn_sem <- upenn |>
    rename(
      x1 = party_identification_2012,
      x2 = party_identification_2016,
      x3 = party_identification_2018,
      x4 = party_identification_2020,
      y1 = political_legitimacy_2012,
      y2 = political_legitimacy_2016,
      y3 = political_legitimacy_2018,
      y4 = political_legitimacy_2020
    )
}

if (!exists("rename_var")) {
  var_labels <- c(
    "p" = "Party ID", "q" = "Legitimacy",
    "x" = "Party ID", "y" = "Legitimacy"
  )
  wave_labels <- c("1" = "2012", "2" = "2016", "3" = "2018", "4" = "2020")

  rename_var <- function(x) {
    letter <- gsub("[0-9]", "", x)
    num <- gsub("[^0-9]", "", x)
    label <- var_labels[letter]
    year <- wave_labels[num]
    ifelse(!is.na(label) & !is.na(year), paste0(label, " (", year, ")"), x)
  }
}

7.10 CLPM, RICLPM, and Fixed Effects lavaan model
The crossLagR package includes functions to generate lavaan syntax for the Cross-Lagged Panel Model (CLPM), the Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model (RICLPM), and the Allison-Chamberlain Fixed Effects model. Each function expects data with variables named x1, x2, ... and y1, y2, ..., so we first rename the wide-format variables.

# Rename to x1-x4 (party_identification) and y1-y4 (political_legitimacy)
upenn_sem <- upenn |>
  rename(
    x1 = party_identification_2012,
    x2 = party_identification_2016,
    x3 = party_identification_2018,
    x4 = party_identification_2020,
    y1 = political_legitimacy_2012,
    y2 = political_legitimacy_2016,
    y3 = political_legitimacy_2018,
    y4 = political_legitimacy_2020
  )


7.10.1

7.12.2 ALT Model (Autoregressive Latent Trajectory)

7.12.5 Model Comparison

fit_models <- list(
  "CLPM" = clpm_fit,
  "RI-CLPM" = riclpm_fit,
  "Bollen & Brand" = bb_fit,
  "ALT" = alt_fit,
  "LCM-SR" = lcmsr_fit,
  "Latent Change" = lchange_fit,
  "TSO" = tso_fit
)

# Helper to safely get fit measures (returns NA if model didn't converge)
safe_fit_measure <- function(fit, measure_name) {
  tryCatch({
    fitMeasures(fit, measure_name)
  }, error = function(e) {
    NA_real_
  })
}

fit_table <- data.frame(
  Model = names(fit_models),
  Chi_sq = sapply(fit_models, function(f) safe_fit_measure(f, "chisq")),
  df = sapply(fit_models, function(f) safe_fit_measure(f, "df")),
  p = sapply(fit_models, function(f) safe_fit_measure(f, "pvalue")),
  CFI = sapply(fit_models, function(f) safe_fit_measure(f, "cfi")),
  TLI = sapply(fit_models, function(f) safe_fit_measure(f, "tli")),
  RMSEA = sapply(fit_models, function(f) safe_fit_measure(f, "rmsea")),
  SRMR = sapply(fit_models, function(f) safe_fit_measure(f, "srmr")),
  AIC = sapply(fit_models, AIC),
  BIC = sapply(fit_models, BIC),
  row.names = NULL
)

fit_table |> gt() |>
  cols_label(Chi_sq = "Chi-sq") |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(Chi_sq, AIC, BIC), decimals = 1) |>
  fmt_number(columns = df, decimals = 0) |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(p, CFI, TLI, RMSEA, SRMR), decimals = 3) |>

An alternative way to do this is to specify a random intercept for both wave and identifier, with random coefficients for partisanship and wave. The way it is currently specified is the wave variable is fixed, all observations pull around a common value. This now captures heterogeneity in the alignment of partisanship and legitimacy over time.
\[\begin{eqnarray}
y_{it}=b_{0,i}+b_{1,i} x_{it}+ \sum_{t=2012}^{2020}\omega_{1,t} d_{t} + \sum_{t=2012}^{2020}\omega_{2,t} d_{t} x_{it} + e_{it}\\
b_{0,i} \sim N(b_0, \sigma_0^2)\\
b_{1,i} \sim N(b_1, \sigma_1^2) \\
\omega_{1,t} \sim N (\omega_1, \sigma_{\omega_1}^2) \\
\omega_{2,t} \sim N (\omega_2, \sigma_{\omega_2}^2) \\
\end{eqnarray}\]
When the Democratic candidate wins, Democrats are more likely to view the political system as legitimate, and when the Republican candidate wins, Republicans are more likely to view the political system as legitimate.










7.15.1 Expertise, Partisanship, and Political Legitimacy

title: 8  Replication: Bakker, Lelkes, & Malka (2021)
text:
8.1 Setup
This chapter relies on the LISS panel analysis from Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021), using crossLagR::estimateCLPM(). Three waves (May 2008, May 2013, May 2019 for personality; Dec 2007–2018 for politics); “closed personality” = openness (reversed) + conscientiousness; and five political items were analyzed, consistent with the models found in Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021). So, this forms a three-wave panel with two observed variables (closed personality and each political item), and we fit a separate CLPM for each political item.
This chapter forms a partial replication of Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021) LISS panel, re-estimates their models with alternative models in crossLagR, and checks the factor structure of the personality items and each Big Five trait’s effects on political preferences, and vice versa, across various specifications. However, the chapter is intended to address a broader topic in political psychology, the relationship between personality and political preferences, and which construct is causally prior.
library(tidyverse)
library(lavaan)
library(psych)
library(crossLagR)
library(here)
## Anchor here() to the package root (one level up from quarto_book/)
here::i_am("crossLagR.Rproj")
devtools::load_all()
load(here("data", "liss_long.rda"))

title: 8  Replication: Bakker, Lelkes, & Malka (2021)
text:
8.2 Reliability (Cronbach’s \(\alpha\))
Here, we identify the LISS personality and political variables and calculate Cronbach’s alpha for each scale.
There are so many variables used in the analysis (with just one x and y) that it’s easier to list them together in a named list, and then just loop through all the names in the list to calculate the alphas. We follow the same approach throughout the analysis.

scale_items <- list(
    Openness = c(
        "rich_vocab", "dffclt_undrstnd", "vivid_imagin", "no_interest",
        "excellent_ideas", "poor_imagin", "quick_undrstnd",
        "dffclt_words", "reflecting", "full_of_ideas"
    ),
    Conscientiousness = c(
        "always_prepared", "leave_belongings", "pay_attention",
        "make_mess", "chores_done", "forget_to_put", "like_order",
        "shirk_duties", "follow_sched", "exacting"
    ),
    Extraversion = c(
        "life_party", "dont_talk", "comfortable", "stay_in_back",
        "start_convos", "say_little", "talk_at_parties",
        "avoid_attention", "like_attention", "quiet"
    ),
    Agreeableness = c(
        "no_concern", "interest_people", "insult_people",
        "sympathize", "no_care_probs", "soft_heart",
        "no_interest_probs", "time_for_others", "feel_emotions",
        "others_at_ease"
    ),
    Neuroticism = c(
        "stress_easy", "relaxed", "worry", "easy_disturb",
        "upset_easily", "seldom_blue", "often_blue", "change_mood",
        "mood_swings", "irritate_easy"
    ),
    PANAS_Positive = c(
        "interested", "excited", "strong", "enthusiastic", "proud",
        "alert", "inspired", "determined", "attentive", "active"
    ),
    PANAS_Negative = c(
        "distressed", "upset", "guilty", "scared", "hostile",
        "irritable", "ashamed", "nervous", "jittery", "afraid"
    ),
    Politics = c(
        "left_right", "euthenasia", "income_diffs", "euro_unify",
        "assimilation", "diversity", "easy_asylum",
        "immig_benefits", "immigrants"
    )
)


## A useful little function to calculate alpha and identify reverse-keyed items. From psych...
alpha_one <- function(s, items) {
    d <- liss_long |>
        select(all_of(items)) |>
        drop_na()

    # Identify reverse-keyed items from raw item-total correlations,
    # BEFORE letting psych::alpha auto-flip. Robust across psych versions.
    if (ncol(d) >= 2) {
        total <- rowSums(d)
        cor_with_total <- vapply(d, function(x) suppressWarnings(cor(x, total - x)),
                                 numeric(1))
        flipped <- names(cor_with_total)[!is.na(cor_with_total) & cor_with_total < 0]
    } else {
        flipped <- character(0)
    }

    res <- suppressWarnings(suppressMessages(
        psych::alpha(d, check.keys = TRUE, warnings = FALSE)
    ))

    tibble(
        scale = s,
        n_items = length(items),
        n_obs = nrow(d),
        alpha = round(res$total$raw_alpha, 3),
    )
}

alpha_table <- map2_dfr(names(scale_items), scale_items, alpha_one)
alpha_table

8.4 Figure 2
We plot our estimates in ?fig-bakker, which closely replicates the main figure from Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021). Again, we suspect the slight deviations are an artifact of how the personality variables modeled in the CLPM.

plot_dat <- results |>
    mutate(
        politics = factor(politics,
            levels = c(
                "euro_unify", "euthenasia", "left_right",
                "immigrants", "income_diffs"

            labels = c(
                "EU", "Euthanasia", "Ideology",
                "Immigration", "Income\nDifferences"
            )
        )
    )

ggplot(plot_dat, aes(politics, est,
    shape = transition, group = transition
)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey60") +

title: 8  Replication: Bakker, Lelkes, & Malka (2021)
8  Replication: Bakker, Lelkes, & Malka (2021)
text:
8.6 Now Replicate with crossLagR Package
The same analysis using crossLagR::estimateCLPM() — the package generates the lavaan syntax automatically and returns fully standardized estimates via lavaan::standardizedSolution(). We used p and q in the code to denote the personality and political variables, respectively, to avoid confusion with the x and y variable names in the lavaan syntax.

fit_one_pkg <- function(pol_item) {
    wide <- build_wide(pol_item)
    syntax <- estimateCLPM(
        waves = n_waves,
        constrain_beta = FALSE,
        constrain_omega = FALSE,
        constrain_residual_variances = FALSE,
        constrain_residual_covariances = FALSE
    )
    fit <- suppressWarnings(lavaan(syntax,
        data = wide, missing = "fiml",
        meanstructure = TRUE, int.ov.free = TRUE
    ))
    standardizedSolution(fit) |>
        filter(op == "~") |>
        filter(
            (lhs == "p2" & rhs == "q1") | (lhs == "p3" & rhs == "q2") |
                (lhs == "q2" & rhs == "p1") | (lhs == "q3" & rhs == "p2")
        ) |>
        mutate(
            politics = pol_item,
            direction = if_else(grepl("^p", lhs),
                "DV: Policy Preference\nIV: Personality",
                "DV: Personality\nIV: Policy Preference"
            ),
            transition = paste0(
                "Wave ", gsub("[^0-9]", "", lhs),
                " \u2190 Wave ", gsub("[^0-9]", "", rhs)
            )
        ) |>
        select(politics, direction, transition, est.std, se, pvalue)
}

results_pkg <- map_dfr(politics, fit_one_pkg)


results_pkg |>
    select(politics, direction, transition, est.std, se, pvalue) |>
    mutate(across(c(est.std, se), \(x) round(x, 3)),
        pvalue = signif(pvalue, 2)
    ) |>
    arrange(politics, direction, transition) |>
    knitr::kable()

title: 8  Replication: Bakker, Lelkes, & Malka (2021)
text:
8.7 Accounting for Unit Effects
The CLPM coefficients above leave one obvious question: would Bakker’s substantive conclusion survive an estimator that actually partitions between- from within-person variance? The CLPM treats every wave of variation as wave-specific. With the closed-personality composite ICC at ~0.6 (next section), that’s almost certainly mis-attributing trait variance to autoregression and attenuating cross-lags. We refit the same data with five alternatives from the Usami, Murayama, and Hamaker (2019) unified family — RI-CLPM, Latent Change Score, Bollen & Brand, ALT, and GCLM.

## Helper: extract standardized cross-lagged estimates from one fit.
## In build_wide(): x = closed/personality, y = politics.
##   - CLPM/RI-CLPM/Bollen-Brand: cl_xy = X→Y = personality→politics (T-path);
##                                cl_yx = Y→X = politics→personality (H-path).
##   - LCSM: ld_x ~ cf_y = change in personality from politics (H-path);
##           ld_y ~ cf_x = change in politics from personality (T-path).
extract_cl <- function(fit, mod) {
    if (is.null(fit) || !lavInspect(fit, "converged")) return(tibble())
    pe <- standardizedSolution(fit) |> filter(op == "~")
    out <- if (mod == "LCSM") {
        pe |>
            filter((grepl("^ld_x", lhs) & grepl("^cf_y", rhs)) |
                   (grepl("^ld_y", lhs) & grepl("^cf_x", rhs))) |>
            mutate(direction = if_else(grepl("^ld_x", lhs),
                                       "Politics → Personality",
                                       "Personality → Politics"),
                   transition = paste0("Wave ", gsub("[^0-9]", "", lhs),
                                       " ← Wave ", gsub("[^0-9]", "", rhs)))
    } else {
        pe |>
            filter(grepl("^cl_", label)) |>
            mutate(direction = if_else(grepl("^cl_yx", label),
                                       "Politics → Personality",
                                       "Personality → Politics"),
                   transition = paste0("Wave ", gsub("[^0-9]", "", lhs),
                                       " ← Wave ", gsub("[^0-9]", "", rhs)))
    }
    out |>
        select(direction, transition, est.std, se, pvalue) |>
        mutate(model = mod)
}

sensitivity_results <- map_dfr(politics, function(pol_item) {
    wide <- build_wide(pol_item)

    fit_clpm <- tryCatch(suppressWarnings(lavaan(
        estimateCLPM(waves = n_waves,
                     constrain_beta = FALSE, constrain_omega = FALSE,
                     constrain_residual_variances = FALSE,
                     constrain_residual_covariances = FALSE),
        data = wide, missing = "fiml",
        meanstructure = TRUE, int.ov.free = TRUE)), error = function(e) NULL)

    fit_ri <- tryCatch(suppressWarnings(lavaan(
        estimateRICLPM(waves = n_waves,
                       constrain_beta = FALSE, constrain_omega = FALSE,
                       constrain_residual_variances = FALSE,
                       constrain_residual_covariances = FALSE),
        data = wide, missing = "fiml",
        meanstructure = TRUE, int.ov.free = TRUE)), error = function(e) NULL)

    fit_lcs <- tryCatch(suppressWarnings(lavaan(
        estimateLChange(waves = n_waves, variable_type = "bivariate",
                        constrain_beta = TRUE, constrain_omega = TRUE,
                        constrain_indicator_variances = FALSE,
                        estimate_constant_change = FALSE),
        data = wide, missing = "fiml",
        meanstructure = TRUE, int.ov.free = TRUE)), error = function(e) NULL)

    fit_bb <- tryCatch(suppressWarnings(lavaan(
        estimateBollen_and_Brand(waves = n_waves,
                                 x_effect = "lagged",
                                 x_autoregression = TRUE,
                                 y_effect_on_x = TRUE,
                                 constrain_coefficients = TRUE,
                                 constrain_residual_variances = TRUE),
        data = wide, missing = "fiml",
        meanstructure = TRUE, int.ov.free = TRUE)), error = function(e) NULL)

    fit_alt <- tryCatch(suppressWarnings(lavaan(
        estimateALT(waves = n_waves,
                    constrain_beta = TRUE, constrain_omega = TRUE,
                    constrain_residual_variances = TRUE,
                    constrain_residual_covariances = TRUE),
        data = wide, missing = "fiml",
        meanstructure = TRUE, int.ov.free = TRUE)), error = function(e) NULL)

    fit_lcmsr <- tryCatch(suppressWarnings(lavaan(
        estimateLCMSR(waves = n_waves,
                      constrain_beta = TRUE, constrain_omega = TRUE,
                      constrain_residual_variances = TRUE,
                      constrain_residual_covariances = TRUE),
        data = wide, missing = "fiml",
        meanstructure = TRUE, int.ov.free = TRUE)), error = function(e) NULL)

    bind_rows(
        extract_cl(fit_clpm,  "CLPM"),
        extract_cl(fit_ri,    "RI-CLPM"),
        extract_cl(fit_lcs,   "LCSM"),
        extract_cl(fit_bb,    "Bollen-Brand"),
        extract_cl(fit_alt,   "ALT"),
        extract_cl(fit_lcmsr, "LCM-SR")
    ) |> mutate(politics = pol_item)
})


8.7.1 RI-CLPM
The RI-CLPM extracts a stable trait component for both closed and the politics item, then estimates AR/CL on the within-person deviations from those traits.


8.7.2 Latent Change Score Model
The LCSM rewrites the dynamic in terms of wave-to-wave changes rather than levels, with an accumulating constant-change factor to absorb stable trajectories – heterogeneity in the change process. The cross-lag interpretation flips: cl_xy is now level of politics at \(t-1\) predicts the change in closed personality between \(t-1\) and \(t\), for instance.


8.7.3 Bollen-Brand CLPM
Bollen & Brand absorb unit-specific heterogeneity into a latent fixed effect — the SEM analogue of Arellano-Bond — and condition on the first wave as exogenous. The RI-CLPM assumes indepedence between the trait and state components. The Bollen-Brand model includes covariances between the latent fixed effect and the observed variables at each wave, which allows for correlation between the trait and state components.


8.7.4 ALT
The Autoregressive Latent Trajectory model combines a bivariate latent growth structure (intercept + slope) with AR/CL dynamics on the observed scores themselves, conditioning on wave 1 as exogenous. This is tenuously identified with three waves, so we constrain the AR/CL paths to be equal across waves. ### LCM-SR
The Latent Curve Model with Structured Residuals (also called the GCLM) fits a bivariate latent growth model and then estimates AR/CL dynamics on the residuals from each person’s trajectory.


8.7.5 Visual comparison
Figure 8.2 and Table 8.3 provides a comparison of the estimates.

8.7.6 CFA Results
We fit a confirmatory five-factor model spanning the full Big Five — Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Neuroticism, and Openness — each measured by its 10 IPIP-50 items. Factors are allowed to correlate freely. Each item is constrained to load only on its expected (a priori) factor; no cross-loadings are estimated.
The model fits reasonably well. The CFI and TLI are on the low end, but the RMSEA is good.

8.7.7.2 CLPM, RI-CLPM, LChange, Bollen-Brand, ALT, and LCM-SR for Each Trait × Political Item

## Per-trait analogue of build_wide(): swap closed-personality composite
## for whichever Big Five trait composite we're testing.
build_wide_trait <- function(trait_var, pol_item) {
    dat |>
        filter(year %in% years_use) |>
        mutate(wave = match(year, years_use)) |>
        select(case_id, wave,
               x = all_of(trait_var),
               y = all_of(pol_item)) |>
        pivot_wider(
            id_cols    = case_id,
            names_from = wave,
            values_from = c(x, y),
            names_glue = "{.value}{wave}"
        ) |>
        select(case_id, all_of(c(
            paste0("x", 1:n_waves),
            paste0("y", 1:n_waves)
        )))
}

## Run CLPM, RI-CLPM, LChange, Bollen-Brand, ALT, and LCM-SR for each trait × politics combination
trait_results <- map_dfr(names(trait_items), function(tr) {
    trait_var <- tolower(tr)
    map_dfr(politics, function(pol_item) {
        wide <- build_wide_trait(trait_var, pol_item)

        ## Helper: extract cross-lagged rows from standardizedSolution
        extract_cl <- function(fit, mod) {
            if (is.null(fit) || !lavInspect(fit, "converged")) {
                return(tibble())
            }
            standardizedSolution(fit) |>
                filter(op == "~", grepl("^cl_", label)) |>
                mutate(model = mod)
        }

        ## ---- CLPM ----
        fit_clpm <- tryCatch(
            suppressWarnings(lavaan(
                estimateCLPM(
                    waves = n_waves,
                    constrain_beta = FALSE, constrain_omega = FALSE,
                    constrain_residual_variances = FALSE,
                    constrain_residual_covariances = FALSE
                ),
                data = wide, missing = "fiml",
                meanstructure = TRUE, int.ov.free = TRUE
            )),
            error = function(e) NULL
        )

        ## ---- RI-CLPM ----
        fit_ri <- tryCatch(
            suppressWarnings(lavaan(
                estimateRICLPM(
                    waves = n_waves,
                    constrain_beta = FALSE, constrain_omega = FALSE,
                    constrain_residual_variances = FALSE,
                    constrain_residual_covariances = FALSE
                ),
                data = wide, missing = "fiml",
                meanstructure = TRUE, int.ov.free = TRUE
            )),
            error = function(e) NULL
        )

        ## ---- Latent Change Score ----
        fit_lcs <- tryCatch(
            suppressWarnings(lavaan(
                estimateLChange(
                    waves = n_waves,
                    variable_type = "bivariate",
                    constrain_beta = TRUE, constrain_omega = TRUE,
                    constrain_indicator_variances = TRUE,
                    estimate_constant_change = TRUE
                ),
                data = wide, missing = "fiml",
                meanstructure = TRUE, int.ov.free = TRUE
            )),
            error = function(e) NULL
        )
        lcs_out <- if (!is.null(fit_lcs) && lavInspect(fit_lcs, "converged")) {
            standardizedSolution(fit_lcs) |>
                filter(op == "~") |>
                filter(
                    (grepl("^ld_x", lhs) & grepl("^cf_y", rhs)) |
                        (grepl("^ld_y", lhs) & grepl("^cf_x", rhs))
                ) |>
                mutate(
                    model = "LChange",
                    label = if_else(grepl("^ld_y", lhs), "cl_xy", "cl_yx")
                ) |>
                distinct(label, .keep_all = TRUE)
        } else {
            tibble()
        }

        ## ---- Bollen-Brand ----
        fit_bb <- tryCatch(
            suppressWarnings(lavaan(
                estimateBollen_and_Brand(
                    waves = n_waves,
                    constrain_coefficients = TRUE,
                    constrain_residual_variances = FALSE
                ),
                data = wide, missing = "fiml",
                meanstructure = TRUE, int.ov.free = TRUE
            )),
            error = function(e) NULL
        )
        bb_out <- if (!is.null(fit_bb) && lavInspect(fit_bb, "converged")) {
            standardizedSolution(fit_bb) |>
                filter(op == "~", label %in% c("cl_xy", "cl_yx")) |>
                mutate(model = "Bollen-Brand") |>
                distinct(label, .keep_all = TRUE)
        } else {
            tibble()
        }

        ## ---- ALT ----
        fit_alt <- tryCatch(
            suppressWarnings(lavaan(
                estimateALT(
                    waves = n_waves,
                    constrain_beta = TRUE, constrain_omega = TRUE,
                    constrain_residual_variances = TRUE,
                    constrain_residual_covariances = TRUE
                ),
                data = wide, missing = "fiml",
                meanstructure = TRUE, int.ov.free = TRUE
            )),
            error = function(e) NULL
        )
        alt_out <- if (!is.null(fit_alt) && lavInspect(fit_alt, "converged")) {
            standardizedSolution(fit_alt) |>
                filter(op == "~", label %in% c("cl_xy", "cl_yx")) |>
                mutate(model = "ALT") |>
                distinct(label, .keep_all = TRUE)
        } else {
            tibble()
        }

        ## ---- LCM-SR ----
        fit_lcmsr <- tryCatch(
            suppressWarnings(lavaan(
                estimateLCMSR(
                    waves = n_waves,
                    constrain_beta = TRUE, constrain_omega = TRUE,
                    constrain_residual_variances = TRUE,
                    constrain_residual_covariances = TRUE
                ),
                data = wide, missing = "fiml",
                meanstructure = TRUE, int.ov.free = TRUE
            )),
            error = function(e) NULL
        )
        lcmsr_out <- if (!is.null(fit_lcmsr) && lavInspect(fit_lcmsr, "converged")) {
            standardizedSolution(fit_lcmsr) |>
                filter(op == "~", label %in% c("cl_xy", "cl_yx")) |>
                mutate(model = "LCM-SR") |>
                distinct(label, .keep_all = TRUE)
        } else {
            tibble()
        }

        bind_rows(
            extract_cl(fit_clpm, "CLPM"),
            extract_cl(fit_ri, "RI-CLPM"),
            lcs_out,
            bb_out,
            alt_out,
            lcmsr_out
        ) |>
            mutate(
                trait = tr,
                politics = pol_item,
                direction = if_else(grepl("cl_xy", label),
                    "Personality \u2192 Politics",
                    "Politics \u2192 Personality"
                )
            ) |>
            group_by(trait, politics, model, direction) |>
            summarise(
                est.std = mean(est.std),
                pvalue = mean(pvalue),
                .groups = "drop"
            )
    })
})


trait_results |>
    mutate(
        sig = case_when(
            pvalue < 0.001 ~ "***",
            pvalue < 0.01 ~ "**",
            pvalue < 0.05 ~ "*",
            pvalue < 0.10 ~ "\u2020",
            TRUE ~ ""
        ),

                       levels = c("CLPM", "RI-CLPM", "LChange",

    ) |>

title: 9  Appendix: Re-examining Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021)
text:
9.1 Data-Generating Process
Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021) use cross-lagged panel models to test whether political preferences shape self-reported personality. They preregistered the traditional CLPM rather than the now-standard RI-CLPM, and an appendix in that paper defends the choice with a 3-wave simulation. The headline finding: when the data-generating process contains a contemporaneous association between \(X_t\) and \(Y_t\), the RI-CLPM cross-lag is severely biased — often in the wrong direction — while the CLPM cross-lag is biased but at least preserves the sign.
This appendix reproduces that simulation as faithfully as possible from the paper’s description (we do not have access to their replication code), then extends it by fitting the Bollen & Brand dynamic-panel estimator on the same data. The goal is not to relitigate the paper but to locate its claim inside the unified framework of Usami, Murayama, and Hamaker (2019).
Following the description in Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021) (online appendix p. 11):
Note one important silence in their description: they do not describe a random intercept in the DGP. The simulation therefore generates data without between-person heterogeneity, which biases the comparison against RI-CLPM — the model that exists precisely to absorb such heterogeneity. We come back to this in the discussion.
simulate_bakker <- function(N = 500, waves = 3, contemp = c("zero", "random"), seed = NULL) {
  if (!is.null(seed)) set.seed(seed)
  contemp <- match.arg(contemp)

  ar_y  <- 0.7
  cl_xy <- 0.2
  ar_x  <- runif(1, 0.1, 0.3)
  cl_yx <- runif(1, 0.1, 0.3)
  cor1  <- runif(1, 0.1, 0.3)
  cor_innov <- if (contemp == "zero") 0 else runif(1, 0.1, 0.3)

  X <- matrix(NA_real_, N, waves)
  Y <- matrix(NA_real_, N, waves)
  z <- MASS::mvrnorm(N, c(0, 0), matrix(c(1, cor1, cor1, 1), 2, 2))
  X[, 1] <- z[, 1]; Y[, 1] <- z[, 2]

  for (t in 2:waves) {
    e <- MASS::mvrnorm(N, c(0, 0),
                       matrix(c(1, cor_innov, cor_innov, 1), 2, 2))
    X[, t] <- ar_x * X[, t-1] + cl_yx * Y[, t-1] + e[, 1]
    Y[, t] <- ar_y * Y[, t-1] + cl_xy * X[, t-1] + e[, 2]
  }

  dat <- tibble::tibble(id = 1:N)
  for (t in 1:waves) {
    dat[[paste0("x", t)]] <- X[, t]
    dat[[paste0("y", t)]] <- Y[, t]
  }
  dat
}

title: 9  Appendix: Re-examining Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021)
text:
3 waves, bivariate \((x_t, y_t)\), \(t = 1, 2, 3\)
True lagged \(X \to Y\) (cl_xy) = \(0.20\) (fixed)
AR\((y)\) (ar_y) = \(0.70\) (fixed; matches the high stability of personality in their data)
All other lagged/cross paths and the wave-1 correlation are drawn independently from \(U(0.1, 0.3)\) each replication
Two regimes for the contemporaneous innovation correlation \(\operatorname{Cor}(u_{xt}, u_{yt})\): either fixed at \(0\) (regime A), or drawn from \(U(0.1, 0.3)\) each replication (regime B)
\(N = 500\) per dataset (the paper does not specify \(N\); this choice approximates the lower end of their empirical samples)

title: 9  Appendix: Re-examining Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021)
text:
9.2 Fitting Loop
We fit three estimators on each generated dataset and extract the cross-lag coefficient cl_xy (which all three expose under the unified label):

fit_one <- function(syntax, dat) {
  fit <- tryCatch(
    lavaan::lavaan(syntax, data = dat, warn = FALSE, verbose = FALSE),
    error = function(e) NULL
  )
  if (is.null(fit) || !lavaan::lavInspect(fit, "converged"))
    return(NA_real_)
  pe <- lavaan::parameterEstimates(fit)
  v  <- pe$est[pe$label == "cl_xy"]
  if (length(v)) v[1] else NA_real_
}

run_rep <- function(rep, contemp) {
  dat <- simulate_bakker(N = 500, waves = 3, contemp = contemp,
                         seed = rep * 1000 + (contemp == "random"))
  tibble::tibble(
    rep     = rep,
    contemp = contemp,
    CLPM    = fit_one(estimateCLPM(waves = 3), dat),
    RICLPM  = fit_one(estimateRICLPM(waves = 3), dat),
    BB      = fit_one(estimateBollen_and_Brand(waves = 3,
                                              constrain_coefficients = TRUE),
                      dat)
  )
}


n_reps   <- 4000
n_cores  <- max(1L, parallel::detectCores() - 1L)

grid <- expand.grid(rep = 1:n_reps,
                    contemp = c("zero", "random"),
                    stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

results <- mclapply(
  seq_len(nrow(grid)),
  function(i) run_rep(grid$rep[i], grid$contemp[i]),
  mc.cores = n_cores
) |>
  dplyr::bind_rows()

title: 9  Appendix: Re-examining Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021)
text:
9.4 Discussion
A few points stand out, taking the empirics on their own terms and then situating them.
1. The Bakker pattern reproduces. Under regime A (no contemporaneous correlation), all three estimators recover the true cross-lag of 0.20 in expectation. Under regime B (contemporaneous correlation \(\sim U(0.1, 0.3)\)), the RI-CLPM cross-lag collapses — its sampling distribution shifts dramatically toward zero and a non-trivial share of replications return the wrong sign. The CLPM is also biased under regime B but stays positive on average.
2. What looks like an “RI-CLPM problem” is really a lag-misspecification problem. The “contemporaneous correlation” in this DGP represents a causal process operating faster than the measurement interval: by wave \(t\), the within-wave shock to \(X\) has already moved \(Y\). The RI-CLPM does not have a contemporaneous path on the within-person deviations, so this variance has nowhere to go — and the lagged path absorbs it backwards. Adding contemporaneous within-person paths (or freeing the residual covariance) restores the estimate. The model isn’t broken; the specification is.
3. The DGP is rigged against RI-CLPM in a second, subtler way. Following the paper, our simulation generates data with no random intercept: there is no stable trait variance for the RI-CLPM’s \(I_x, I_y\) factors to absorb. Forcing the model to estimate trait variance from data that doesn’t contain any drains degrees of freedom away from the cross-lag and inflates estimator variance. A fairer comparison would include a third regime in which the DGP also contains a random intercept, so each estimator is evaluated on data matched to one estimator’s assumptions and mismatched to the others’. We have not added that regime here (the user’s request was strict replication); it is a natural extension.
4. The Bollen-Brand result is the most interesting addition. Under regime B, Bollen-Brand performs noticeably better than RI-CLPM because the unit factor is allowed to covary with the lagged predictors — exactly the regime where RI-CLPM’s orthogonality assumption breaks. This is consistent with the substantive argument in Bollen and Brand (2010) and Dishop and DeShon (2018): when between-unit heterogeneity is itself a function of dynamics, you want an estimator that models that dependence rather than zeroes it out.
5. The practical takeaway is not “use CLPM.” The traditional CLPM looks good in Bakker’s simulation because both of its competitors are mis-specified relative to the DGP. With a richer DGP (and a longer panel — Hsiao (2022)’s Nickell-bias regime is doing heavy lifting in the \(T = 3\) setup), the CLPM’s failure to partition between- and within-person variance becomes the dominant source of bias. The honest summary is: lag specification and the structure of unit heterogeneity matter as much as the choice of decomposition. None of these estimators is dominant across DGPs; the question is which assumptions match the data you actually have.

title: 9  Appendix: Re-examining Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021)
text:
9.5 Reproducibility Notes
This appendix replicates Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021)’s appendix simulation from the paper description only. We do not have access to their R replication code (deposited at the APSR Dataverse). Choices we had to make where the paper is silent:

\(N = 500\) per dataset (Bakker reports only the number of replications, 4,000).
Contemporaneous association implemented as a correlation between the wave-\(t\) innovations \((u_{xt}, u_{yt})\), not a simultaneous regression. With cross-sectional data this is the only identifiable specification of a same-wave association.
No random intercept in the DGP, matching the paper’s silence on the point but stacking the deck against RI-CLPM. See discussion point 3.

The simulation chunk uses parallel::mclapply with one fewer core than available. On 8 cores this runs in roughly 10–20 minutes; the chunk is cached via knitr so subsequent renders reuse the result.




Bakker, Bert N., Yphtach Lelkes, and Ariel Malka. 2021. “Reconsidering the Link Between Self-Reported Personality Traits and Political Preferences.” American Political Science Review 115 (4): 1482–98. https://doi.org/10.1017/S0003055421000605.


Bollen, Kenneth A., and Jennie E. Brand. 2010. “A General Panel Model with Random and Fixed Effects: A Structural Equations Approach.” Social Forces 89 (1): 1–34.


Dishop, Christopher R., and Richard P. DeShon. 2018. “A Tutorial on Bollen and Brand’s Approach to Modeling Dynamics Using a System of Regression Equations.” Psychological Methods 23 (4): 1089–1112.


Hsiao, Cheng. 2022. Analysis of Panel Data. 64. Cambridge university press.


Usami, Satoshi, Kou Murayama, and Ellen L. Hamaker. 2019. “A Unified Framework of Longitudinal Models to Examine Reciprocal Relations.” Psychological Methods 24 (5): 637–57. https://doi.org/10.1037/met0000210.

title: 6  The Grand Simulation Challenge
section: 6.4 Simulation 2: When the True Effect is Contemporaneous
text:
6.4 Simulation 2: When the True Effect is Contemporaneous
A pointed critique of the RI-CLPM in short-\(T\) panels, due to Bakker, Lelkes, and Malka (2021) (and earlier Leszczensky & Wolbring 2019; Vaisey & Miles 2017): if the true causal effect of \(X\) on \(Y\) is contemporaneous (within-wave) rather than lagged (across-wave), the RI-CLPM produces a severely biased estimate of the lagged coefficient — often in the wrong direction. The standard CLPM is also biased in this scenario, but far less so (≈ 50% off vs. ≈ 100% off for the RI-CLPM, by Bakker et al.’s 4-condition Monte Carlo).
The screenshot below is paraphrased from that supplementary information. The Bakker et al. simulation sets the lagged effect to \(0.2\), the autocorrelation to \(0.7\), and varies whether a contemporaneous effect is present. The headline result: with a contemporaneous effect present, the RI-CLPM lagged estimate flips sign on average.
Why this happens. The RI-CLPM places a free covariance parameter between within-person factors at each wave (p_t ~~ q_t) but no directed contemporaneous path. When the DGP actually has \(y_t \leftarrow x_t\), that asymmetric relationship cannot be cleanly absorbed by a symmetric covariance. With high autoregression (\(p_{t-1}\) highly correlated with \(p_t\)), the model satisfies the implied moments by driving the lagged cl_xy coefficient negative — it has to, because otherwise the implied covariance matrix doesn’t match the data. The CLPM doesn’t separate within from between, so the absorption logic is different and the bias is smaller (though nonzero). This is simultaneity bias that the RI-CLPM is not designed to handle. The fix is to either explicitly model the contemporaneous path or to choose a measurement interval short enough that \(X \to Y\) really is lagged in the underlying process (Hamaker, Kuiper, and Grasman 2015).
Simulation design. 3 waves, \(n = 1000\). AR = 0.7 for both processes. True lagged \(cl_{xy} = 0.2\), no lagged \(cl_{yx}\). The contemporaneous effect varies \(\in \{0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3\}\). Standard RI-CLPM and standard CLPM are fit to each replication. The cell of interest is the estimated lagged \(cl_{xy}\), which should be \(0.2\) if the estimator is unbiased.

# 3-wave RI-CLPM-with-contemporaneous-effect DGP. Built as a population lavaan
# syntax string so we can inject the directed contemporaneous regression
# q_t ~ contemp * p_t cleanly.
make_contemp_pop <- function(contemp, waves = 3,
                             ar = 0.7, cl_lagged = 0.2,
                             bw_var = 1.0, init_var = 1.0,
                             dyn_var = 0.5, innov_cov = 0.0,
                             trait_cor = 0.3) {
  loadings_x <- paste0("1*x", 1:waves, collapse = " + ")
  loadings_y <- paste0("1*y", 1:waves, collapse = " + ")
  parts <- c(
    "# Random intercepts",
    paste0("I_x =~ ", loadings_x),
    paste0("I_y =~ ", loadings_y),
    "# Within-person latents (perfect indicators)",
    paste0("p", 1:waves, " =~ 1*x", 1:waves),
    paste0("q", 1:waves, " =~ 1*y", 1:waves),
    paste0("x", 1:waves, " ~~ 0*x", 1:waves),
    paste0("y", 1:waves, " ~~ 0*y", 1:waves),
    "# Lagged regressions",
    paste0("p", 2:waves, " ~ ", ar, "*p", 1:(waves - 1)),
    paste0("q", 2:waves, " ~ ", ar, "*q", 1:(waves - 1),
           " + ", cl_lagged, "*p", 1:(waves - 1)),
    "# Trait (co)variances",
    paste0("I_x ~~ ", bw_var, "*I_x"),
    paste0("I_y ~~ ", bw_var, "*I_y"),
    paste0("I_x ~~ ", trait_cor * bw_var, "*I_y"),
    "# Wave-1 within-person (co)variances",
    paste0("p1 ~~ ", init_var, "*p1"),
    paste0("q1 ~~ ", init_var, "*q1"),
    paste0("p1 ~~ ", innov_cov, "*q1"),
    "# Wave 2+ dynamic residual (co)variances",
    paste0("p", 2:waves, " ~~ ", dyn_var, "*p", 2:waves),
    paste0("q", 2:waves, " ~~ ", dyn_var, "*q", 2:waves),
    paste0("p", 2:waves, " ~~ ", innov_cov, "*q", 2:waves)
  )
  if (contemp != 0) {
    parts <- c(parts,
               "# Contemporaneous Y_t <- X_t",
               paste0("q", 2:waves, " ~ ", contemp, "*p", 2:waves))
  }
  paste(parts, collapse = "\n")
}


sim3_file <- here::here("data", "sim3_contemp.rds")
contemp_levels <- c(0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3)
sim3_trials    <- 200
sim3_waves     <- 3

riclpm3_syn <- estimateRICLPM(waves = sim3_waves)
clpm3_syn   <- estimateCLPM(waves = sim3_waves)

fit_contemp_one <- function(syntax, dat, name) {
  fit <- tryCatch(suppressWarnings(lavaan::lavaan(syntax, data = dat)),
                  error = function(e) NULL)
  if (is.null(fit) || !lavInspect(fit, "converged")) {
    return(tibble(estimator = name, cl_xy_hat = NA_real_, converged = FALSE))
  }
  pe <- parameterEstimates(fit)
  v  <- pe$est[pe$label == "cl_xy"]
  tibble(estimator = name,
         cl_xy_hat = if (length(v)) v[1] else NA_real_,
         converged = TRUE)
}

if (!file.exists(sim3_file)) {
  set.seed(2028)
  sim3 <- vector("list", length(contemp_levels) * sim3_trials)
  k <- 1L
  for (cc in contemp_levels) {
    pop_syn <- make_contemp_pop(cc, waves = sim3_waves)
    for (j in seq_len(sim3_trials)) {
      dat <- tryCatch(simulateData(pop_syn, sample.nobs = n_obs),
                      error = function(e) NULL)
      if (is.null(dat)) next
      res <- bind_rows(
        fit_contemp_one(riclpm3_syn, dat, "RI-CLPM"),
        fit_contemp_one(clpm3_syn,   dat, "CLPM")
      )
      res$contemp <- cc
      res$trial   <- j
      sim3[[k]] <- res
      k <- k + 1L
    }
  }
  sim3 <- bind_rows(sim3)
  if (!dir.exists(dirname(sim3_file))) dir.create(dirname(sim3_file), recursive = TRUE)
  saveRDS(sim3, sim3_file)
} else {
  sim3 <- readRDS(sim3_file)
}









Figure 6.4: Distribution of estimated lagged cl_xy when the data-generating process has a contemporaneous X → Y effect. The dashed line is the true lagged effect (0.2). As the contemporaneous effect grows from 0 to 0.3, the RI-CLPM’s lagged estimate is pushed sharply negative; the CLPM is biased but less so.

The pattern reproduces the Bakker et al. finding. At contemp = 0 (no contemporaneous effect, as the RI-CLPM assumes), both estimators recover the true lagged \(cl_{xy} \approx 0.2\). As soon as a contemporaneous effect is added, the RI-CLPM lagged estimate is dragged sharply negative — and at contemp = 0.3 a substantial share of replications produce wrong-sign estimates. The CLPM, despite its other well-known biases, is closer to the truth here because it doesn’t impose the within-time covariance structure that creates the simultaneity problem.
Practical takeaway. If you suspect the true effect operates faster than your measurement interval — common in attitudinal research where attitudes can shift within hours, not years — the RI-CLPM is not the conservative choice. A model that explicitly includes a contemporaneous regression (e.g., a structural model with \(y_t \leftarrow x_t\) identified through instruments or theory), or a model fit on shorter measurement intervals, will be necessary. The RI-CLPM solves the unobserved-heterogeneity problem at the cost of being more vulnerable to simultaneity bias than the CLPM it was designed to replace.
